worth_it_explanation: Floor strategy sets the price below which an impression goes unsold, so it controls the trade between fill rate and the value of each impression. A floor that leans entirely on automation, or one flat number applied across every placement, format, and market, can leave revenue uncollected without anyone noticing. The check is a review of the floor rules a publisher already runs on Equativ and in the header-bidding wrapper, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a concrete pricing decision the publisher can test.
intro: Floor pricing decides the minimum price a sell-side platform accepts for an impression in the auction, which sets where the line falls between filling more impressions and protecting the value of each one. This subcategory is about the floor strategy itself, the values chosen and how they are applied across Equativ and the header-bidding setup that feeds it. These checks confirm that the floor strategy is a deliberate, evidence-based choice rather than automation accepted by default, and that the floors reflect the price differences across the publisher's inventory.

audit_checks.title: Floors are not segmented by inventory, format, geography, or device
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A single flat floor across all inventory ignores that some placements, formats, countries, and devices clear at higher prices than others. A flat floor either underprices the strong inventory, leaving revenue uncollected, or overprices the weak inventory, leaving impressions unsold, so one number cannot serve inventory whose value varies along several lines at once.

educate.base:
A floor price works best when it matches what the impression is worth, and impressions are not worth the same across a publisher's inventory. An above-the-fold display unit on a high-engagement page clears higher than a unit far down a thin page. A video or rich-media format clears higher than a small banner. Traffic from a high-spending market clears higher than traffic from a market where advertiser demand is thin. Desktop, mobile web, and in-app inventory each carry their own demand and their own typical clearing price. A floor set as one flat number across all of this cannot fit inventory whose value varies along every one of those lines.

The cost of a flat floor cuts both ways. Set it to the average and it sits below the strong inventory, so impressions that could have cleared higher clear at the floor and the difference is left uncollected. Set it higher to protect the strong inventory and it sits above the weak inventory, so impressions that would have sold at a lower price go unsold instead. Either way, one number serves the middle and misprices the ends.

Equativ supports floor rules at the level of the placement, format, and audience or geography, so a publisher can carry different floors for different slices of inventory rather than one floor for everything. Because Equativ is Prebid-compatible, the same idea applies in the header-bidding wrapper, where the Prebid price floors module assigns floors by dimensions such as ad unit, media type, ad size, and domain. The audit question is whether the floors in place, on the server side and in the wrapper, reflect those value differences or whether a single flat floor is doing work that several segmented floors should do.

A few signs point to floors that are not segmented:
  • One floor value covers all inventory, with no breakout by placement, format, geography, or device, in Equativ or in the Prebid floor rules
  • Reporting shows average clearing prices that differ widely across placements, formats, countries, or devices while the floor stays the same for all of them
  • Premium placements clear at or near the same floor as filler inventory, which suggests the floor is not protecting their value
You may want to confirm that the floors reflect the price differences the inventory actually shows. One approach is to read average clearing price by placement, format, geography, and device, then set floors that follow those differences rather than a single number across the board.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, a homepage or section-front placement usually clears higher than an article-footer unit, video clears higher than display, and audience from high-demand markets clears higher than audience from thin ones. You may want to set floors by placement, format, and geography so the premium inventory holds its value while the lower-value inventory keeps a floor low enough to stay filled.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, product and category pages reach an audience closer to a purchase and clear higher than editorial pages, and device matters because mobile shoppers and desktop shoppers carry different demand. You may want to segment floors so the commercial, higher-intent inventory carries a higher floor than the editorial inventory rather than sharing one number.

learn_more.source_title: Price Floors Module for Header Bidding

educate.learn_more_note: Equativ's detailed floor-rule configuration documentation is in the partner portal (login required). The Prebid and OpenRTB references above cover the floor mechanics that apply across a Prebid-compatible setup.

investigate.intro: Review the floor values across placements, formats, geographies, and device categories, on both Equativ and the header-bidding wrapper, to see whether they are differentiated or whether one flat floor covers everything.

equativ_console.label: Equativ console
steps: Open the inventory or pricing section and list the floor values applied to each placement, format, and audience or geography segment.
Record whether the floors differ across placement, format, geography, and device, or whether a single floor applies across all inventory with no breakout.
Note any premium placement that carries the same floor as standard inventory, which points to a floor that is not protecting its value.

prebid_floors.label: Prebid floor rules
steps: If the publisher runs header bidding, open the Prebid price floors configuration and read the floor rules and the dimensions they target, such as ad unit, media type, ad size, and domain.
Record whether the rules assign different floors by dimension or whether a single catch-all floor covers every ad unit.
Compare the wrapper floors against the Equativ server-side floors so the two are not working against each other.

ssp_reporting.label: Equativ reporting
steps: Build a report broken out by placement, then by format, then by country, then by device category, with average clearing price (eCPM) and fill rate.
Compare the average clearing price across those dimensions to see how much it varies between strong and weak inventory.
Note any case where clearing prices differ widely but the floor stays the same, which points to inventory that a segmented floor could price more accurately.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the floor values across placements, formats, geographies, and device categories, on Equativ and in the Prebid wrapper, alongside the average clearing price for each. Floors that follow the value differences the reporting shows, with higher floors on inventory that clears higher and lower floors on inventory that clears lower, pass. A single flat floor applied across inventory whose clearing prices differ widely is a fail recorded with the dimensions where the floor and the value diverge.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the floor values and the average clearing price by placement, format, geography, and device above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} applies a single flat floor across inventory whose value differs by placement, format, geography, and device. A flat floor either underprices the strong inventory, leaving revenue uncollected, or overprices the weak inventory, leaving impressions unsold, so one number cannot fit inventory that clears at different prices. {{voice_recommend}} reading the average clearing price by placement, format, geography, and device, then setting floor rules in Equativ and the Prebid wrapper that carry higher floors on the inventory that clears higher and lower floors on the inventory that clears lower. After the change, recheck fill rate and average price across the segments to confirm the floors fit the value of each.

audit_checks.title: Dynamic floors are accepted without evaluating them against a fixed-floor baseline
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Dynamic or unified floors let an automated model set the floor per request, which often raises revenue, but the model optimizes toward yield and that objective does not always match a publisher's own strategy, such as protecting a premium price point. Accepting dynamic floors without comparing them to a fixed-floor baseline leaves the choice untested, so a publisher cannot tell whether the automated floor is helping or clearing inventory below the price they meant to protect.

educate.base:
Equativ and the header-bidding wrapper both offer more than one way to set a floor. A fixed floor is a number the publisher chooses and the platform applies as written. A dynamic floor instead hands the value to an automated model that adjusts it per request to balance fill and price, and unified or optimized floor approaches work the same way by setting the floor around a moving target rather than a static number. The automated options often raise revenue, and a model trained on recent auction data can find a floor that fills the impression without giving the inventory away.

The risk is not that automation is wrong. The risk is accepting it without a comparison. An automated floor optimizes toward the objective it was built for, which is yield, and that objective does not always match a publisher's own strategy. A publisher protecting a premium price point on a sponsorship-adjacent placement, or holding a deliberate floor to keep a direct-sold rate intact, may find the dynamic floor clears impressions below the price they meant to protect. Without a baseline to measure against, the publisher has no way to see that happening.

The header-bidding setup supports running this comparison directly. The Prebid price floors module can A/B test more than one floor model in the same auction through its second schema, sampling each model by a set weight, so a publisher can read how a dynamic model performs against a fixed-floor baseline rather than switching everything over on faith. On the Equativ side, a publisher can apply a dynamic floor to a slice of inventory, hold a fixed floor on a comparable slice, and read revenue, fill rate, and average price across both. Either route turns the floor choice into a measured result rather than a default that was never questioned.

A few signs point to dynamic floors being accepted rather than evaluated:
  • Dynamic, unified, or optimized floors run across all inventory with no fixed-floor comparison on record
  • No experiment or before-and-after read exists showing how the dynamic floor performed against a fixed baseline
  • The publisher cannot say why the dynamic option was chosen beyond it being the default or a vendor recommendation
You may want to confirm that the dynamic-versus-fixed choice was made on evidence rather than by default. One approach is to hold a fixed-floor baseline on part of the inventory, or weight a fixed-floor model in the Prebid A/B test, long enough to compare it against the dynamic floor on the rest.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, premium placements such as a homepage takeover slot or a sponsorship-adjacent unit often carry a price the publisher set on purpose to protect direct-sold value. You may want to keep a fixed-floor baseline on those units while testing a dynamic floor elsewhere, so the comparison shows whether automation respects the premium or clears below it.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, product and category pages can command higher prices than editorial pages because the audience is closer to a purchase. You may want to compare a dynamic floor against a fixed floor on the commercial pages specifically, rather than judging it on a blended average that the lower-value editorial inventory pulls down.

learn_more.source_title: Price Floors Module for Header Bidding
Price Floors for Header Bidding (Prebid Server)

educate.learn_more_note: Equativ's detailed dynamic-floor configuration documentation is in the partner portal (login required). The Prebid references above cover the dynamic-versus-fixed floor mechanics and the A/B test that applies across a Prebid-compatible setup.

investigate.intro: Review which floors run dynamic versus fixed, on Equativ and in the Prebid wrapper, and look for any test result or before-and-after read that compares them.

equativ_console.label: Equativ console
steps: Open the pricing or floor configuration and note, per slice of inventory, whether the floor is fixed or dynamic, unified, or optimized.
Record which slices of inventory run on a dynamic floor and which, if any, run on a fixed floor that could serve as a comparison baseline.
Ask the publisher whether any experiment or before-and-after comparison was run, and record what the result showed and when it was measured.

prebid_floors.label: Prebid floor rules
steps: If the publisher runs header bidding, open the Prebid price floors configuration and note whether it uses a dynamic floor model fetched from a provider or a fixed package of floor rules.
Check whether the floors module runs a second-schema A/B test that weights more than one floor model, which would let a fixed baseline run alongside the dynamic model.
Record whether a fixed-floor model is in the test, and at what weight, so a comparison is possible.

ssp_reporting.label: Equativ reporting
steps: Build a report broken out by the floor model or the inventory it targets, with revenue, impressions, fill rate, and average eCPM.
Compare a slice running a dynamic floor against a comparable slice on a fixed floor, if one exists, to see whether the dynamic floor raised revenue or cleared at a lower price.
Note whether the data supports the current floor choice or whether no fixed-floor slice exists to compare against.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per slice of inventory, whether the floor is fixed or dynamic, and whether any comparison between a dynamic floor and a fixed baseline exists with a result on record. A setup where the dynamic-versus-fixed choice is deliberate and backed by a comparison passes. A setup where dynamic floors are applied across inventory with no fixed-floor comparison and no stated reason for the choice is a fail recorded with the inventory affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the floor type for each slice of inventory and whether a dynamic-versus-fixed comparison exists above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} applies dynamic floor pricing without a fixed-floor comparison on record, so the choice between automated and fixed floors has not been tested. A dynamic floor sets the value to maximize yield, which often helps, but that objective does not always match a publisher's own strategy, such as protecting a premium price point, and without a baseline the difference goes unmeasured. {{voice_recommend}} holding a fixed-floor baseline on a slice of comparable inventory, or weighting a fixed-floor model in the Prebid A/B test, running the dynamic floor on the rest, and comparing revenue, fill rate, and average price across both before settling on a floor strategy. After the comparison, keep whichever floor the data supports for each slice of inventory.
